title: p-adic Hodge-theoretic properties of etale cohomology with mod p coefficients, and the cohomology of Shimura varieties - 2
speaker: Toby Gee

proof_stages.label: Main vanishing theorem and generic hypotheses
proof_stages.summary: The speaker states the target vanishing theorem for mod-p étale cohomology of GU(n-1,1) Shimura varieties localized at a Hecke maximal ideal, under irreducibility, p-size, local regularity, non-character-sum, and big-image assumptions on the associated Galois representation.

proof_stages.label: Inertial-weight bounds as the basic obstruction
proof_stages.summary: Caruso's theorem for proper semistable varieties is recalled to bound inertial weights in mod-p cohomology, and the global strategy is identified as contradicting these small weights with the large or separated inertial weights forced by the local Galois hypotheses.

proof_stages.label: Descent-data and determinant obstruction from the modular-curve model
proof_stages.summary: The modular-curve calculations show that at p-level one must retain tame descent data after base change to obtain useful restrictions, and that comparing determinants already gives a sufficiently robust contradiction under genericity.

proof_stages.label: General local p-adic Hodge theorem with tame descent data
proof_stages.summary: Using strongly divisible lattices and Breuil modules with tame descent data, the speaker formulates the general local theorem: a mod-p subrepresentation with the specified determinant inside a potentially semistable representation with Hodge–Tate weights in [0,r] cannot be r-regular under the stated numerical and non-split hypotheses.

proof_stages.label: Hecke localization compared with Galois occurrence
proof_stages.summary: Eichler–Shimura relations and a finite-group representation-theoretic criterion, valid under the big-image or induction hypotheses, are used to pass from nonvanishing of localized Hecke cohomology to actual occurrence of the Galois representation rho_m.

proof_stages.label: Level reduction and Hochschild–Serre induction
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem is concluded by induction on cohomological degree, using Hochschild–Serre spectral sequences to pass to sufficiently small pro-p/Iwahori level and then back to arbitrary level, where p-group invariants reduce nonvanishing to the local contradiction.

examples.name: H^1 of a semistable variety and the missing cyclotomic square
examples.note: For an unramified semistable proper variety, H^1 can have only trivial or mod-p cyclotomic inertia, illustrating Caruso's inertial-weight bound by ruling out a cyclotomic-square character.
examples.engagement_reason: he applies the inertial-weight theorem to H^1 of a semistable proper variety, deriving that only weights 0 and 1 occur and hence the cyclotomic character squared cannot occur

passes.reason: An ordinary semistable H^1 case illustrating Caruso’s inertial-weight bound, with omega^2 merely excluded rather than used to refute a separate claim.

examples.name: Prime-to-p modular curve X_1(N)
examples.note: The n=2, good-level modular-curve case makes concrete how local inertial weights of a modular-form Galois representation conflict with Caruso bounds in H^0 and mostly in H^1.
examples.engagement_reason: he analyzes X_1(N) cohomology by computing the inertial weights of the associated modular-form representation and using Caruso’s bound to conclude nonappearance in H^0 and in H^1 except weight 2

passes.reason: The prime-to-p modular curve X_1(N) is a standard member of a systematic family used as a model case.

examples.name: Iwahori-level modular curve X_1(Np) with nebentype
examples.note: The Gamma_1(Np) calculation shows why one must retain tame descent data after passing to Q_p(zeta_p), and it visibly leads to the determinant-only obstruction that is then generalized.
examples.engagement_reason: he runs the Iwahori-level/descent-data argument for X_1(Np), lists the possible inertia characters from Raynaud’s classification, and compares them with the nebentype and determinant to get restrictions

passes.reason: The Iwahori-level modular curve X_1(Np) with nebentype is a standard level-structure example used to generalize the argument.

examples.name: Characters 1 and omega^2 failing 3-regularity
examples.note: The pair of inertial characters 1 and omega^2 is used only to make the definition of r-regularity concrete, since translating weights by j=0,1,2,3 creates a collision.
examples.engagement_reason: he checks the r-regularity definition on the concrete pair of characters, noting that for 3-regularity one cannot have both 1 and omega squared or omega cubed

examples.name: A_4 three-dimensional representation counterexample
examples.note: The irreducible three-dimensional representation of A_4 illustrates that the desired characteristic-polynomial annihilation criterion is false for n>2 without extra image hypotheses, motivating the big-image condition.
examples.engagement_reason: he gives the A_4 irreducible three-dimensional representation as a counterexample and states its killing-property, but does not compute or verify it in the talk
